Extra
6.Tables7.Markdown Syntax

Type to search titles, headings, and page content.

Search is ready. Try a page title, a section heading, or a phrase from the content.

Directives

Directives are the part of Marque that turns a markdown document into a proper page.

Use markdown for content. Use directives for structure, reusable UI blocks, and project-specific components.

The authoring stack

Markdown

Use plain markdown for headings, paragraphs, lists, links, blockquotes, and code blocks.

Directives

Use directives when you want rows, cards, callouts, steps, wrappers, or custom components.

Inline syntax

Use button links, badges, inline dividers, inline project directives, and <aside class="mq-summary-panel" data-mq-toc data-mq-summary-scope="page" role="navigation" aria-label="Summary"><p class="mq-summary-empty">Add headings to the page to populate this summary.</p></aside> when a full block would be overkill.

How directive syntax works

Block directives

Block directives open with @name and close with @end name.

mq
@card .primary
  ## Title
  Body copy.
@end card

Modifiers and labels

Modifiers are the .tokens after the directive name. Some directives also accept a trailing label.

  • @callout .warn
  • @product-card .featured Starter
  • @dropdown "Windows Users"

Inline directives

Inline directives do not need a closing tag. The built-in divider and the starter's decorative inline directives fall into this group.

Built-in directives

Layout and flow

  • @container: neutral grouping scope that resets step numbering
  • @row: responsive grid based on the number of block children
  • @column: vertical stack inside a row

Wrappers and surfaces

  • @section: themed section wrapper
  • @hero: prominent section wrapper
  • @card: padded content block

Content blocks

  • @callout: highlighted note block
  • @stat: value + label pair
  • @step: numbered process item

Callout variants

@callout supports these built-in variants:

  • info
  • warn
  • danger
  • ok
  • primary
  • secondary
  • tertiary

Inline affordances

Marque also supports lighter syntax in normal markdown:

  • button-style links for CTA-like links inside paragraphs
  • badges for short labels and status tags
  • inline dividers inside running text

Starter project examples

The starter also ships with example project directives:

  • dropdown
  • product-card
  • toc
  • sparkle
  • spotlight

Example composition

mq
@container
  @callout .info
  Directives are just HTML generators with friendlier authoring syntax.
  @end callout

  @row
    @card
      ## Cards
      Great for grouped content.
    @end card

    @card
      ## Layout
      `@row` turns sibling blocks into a grid.
    @end card
  @end row

  @step
  Write the source.
  @end step

  @step
  Let Marque render the HTML.
  @end step
@end container

Directives are just HTML generators with friendlier authoring syntax.

Cards

Great for grouped content.

Layout

@row turns sibling blocks into a grid.

Write the source.

Let Marque render the HTML.

Custom directives

Where they live

Put project directives in directives/**/*.js.

Marque reloads them on each build and on marque serve, so you do not need to patch the parser core to add your own components.

What a directive can define

  • type: block or inline
  • render(...): returns HTML
  • validate(...): optional diagnostics
  • style: optional CSS chunk injected into the generated theme CSS
js
module.exports = ({ defineDirective }) => {
  defineDirective('product-card', {
    type: 'block',
    style: `
product-card {
  display: block;
  border: 1px solid var(--mq-border);
  padding: 1rem;
}
`,
    render: ({ mods, name, children, ctx }) => {
      const variant = ctx.escapeAttr(mods[0] || 'default');
      const title = ctx.escapeAttr(name || '');
      return `<product-card variant="${variant}" title="${title}">${children}</product-card>`;
    },
  });

  defineDirective('sparkle', {
    type: 'inline',
    render: () => '<span class="mq-sparkle" aria-hidden="true">✶</span>',
  });
};

Custom directives can be tiny. If all you need is "wrap the children in an element and style it", that is already enough.

Notes worth knowing

  • @container is structural. It does not emit its own wrapper element.
  • @step numbering resets inside a new container flow.
  • @step 4, @step *, and @step reset:1 are valid when you need manual control.
  • @row expects block directive children like @card or @column.
  • @callout warns on unknown variants.
  • Hyphenated names like product-card are supported.
  • Buttons and badges are authoring syntax, not block directives.
  • Directive styles can ship with the directive itself through style.
  • The starter directives are examples, not sacred. Delete or replace them freely.

Built with Marque
